title: Window Functions: Trends Without Losing the Row
learningObjectives: Compute a rolling average or running total without collapsing rows the way GROUP BY does
Use OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...) to define a window per group in a defined order
Rank or number rows within a group using ROW_NUMBER() or RANK()
Explain why a window function is the right tool when you need both the detail row and a computed trend alongside it
whyItMatters: A daily average (from the Aggregation lesson) collapses an entire day into one number — useful for a summary, useless if you need to see each individual reading alongside a rolling 3-hour average to spot a trend forming in real time. This is the direct SQL precursor to the rolling/resampling operations Mission 3's pandas lessons formalize.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's trend-detection queries — a rolling average alongside every individual reading, the SQL-level version of the smoothing Mission 3's pandas and forecasting work will build on.

problem.summary: GROUP BY collapses exactly the individual rows a trend view needs.
problem.content: You want to see each individual demand reading alongside a rolling 3-hour average, to spot a trend forming — but GROUP BY collapses rows into one summary per group, which throws away exactly the individual rows you need to see.

motivation.summary: 'Show me the trend as it develops' is a genuinely different need from 'summarize the day.'
motivation.content: This is the gap between 'summarize the day' (Aggregation) and 'show me the trend as it develops, row by row' — a genuinely different, very common need once you're looking for patterns rather than single totals.

theory.summary: Window functions compute per-row values over a related set of rows without collapsing them.
theory.content: A window function computes a value across a set of related rows (the 'window') without collapsing them into fewer rows, unlike GROUP BY. `AVG(demand_kw) OVER (PARTITION BY source_id ORDER BY timestamp ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)` computes a rolling 3-row average per source, ordered by time, while still returning one row per original reading. `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY source_id ORDER BY demand_kw DESC)` numbers each source's readings from highest to lowest, useful for 'the top 3 readings per substation' type questions that GROUP BY alone can't express.

visualExplanation.summary: A sliding window moves down an ordered sequence, attaching a rolling value to each current row.
visualExplanation.content: A sequence of individual rows shows a sliding 3-row window highlighted as it moves down the ordered sequence, each position computing and attaching its own rolling average value to the current row rather than replacing it.

interactiveDemo.summary: Adjust a window size and watch the rolling-average column update per row live.
interactiveDemo.content: Adjust a window size slider (e.g. 2-row vs. 5-row rolling average) over a live sequence of readings and watch the computed rolling-average column update per row in real time.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: compute a rolling average alongside every individual row.
guidedCoding.content:
```sql
SELECT
    source_id,
    timestamp,
    demand_kw,
    AVG(demand_kw) OVER (
        PARTITION BY source_id
        ORDER BY timestamp
        ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS rolling_avg_3
FROM readings
ORDER BY source_id, timestamp;
```

independentCoding.summary: Rank readings per source and filter to the top 3, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Write a query that numbers each source's readings by `demand_kw` descending using `ROW_NUMBER()`, then filters to only the top 3 per source using that numbering.

debugging.summary: Recognize when GROUP BY was the wrong tool and rewrite using OVER().
debugging.content: A query uses `GROUP BY` for a task that needed a per-row rolling average, and the individual reading rows got collapsed away, losing exactly the detail the task required. Identify why `GROUP BY` was the wrong tool and rewrite using a window function.

reflection.summary: GROUP BY throws detail away; window functions preserve it alongside the computed value.
reflection.content: What's the real difference between what GROUP BY throws away and what a window function preserves, and why did that distinction matter for this specific task?

teachBack.summary: Explain why a window function can select the raw column and its own aggregate together.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why `AVG(demand_kw) OVER (...)` can appear in the same SELECT as `demand_kw` itself, when a GROUP BY query could never do that.

miniProject.summary: Add a rolling average column to the earlier report without losing detail rows.
miniProject.content: Add a rolling 3-hour average column to the Digital Twin's readings report from the Aggregation lesson, without losing any individual reading rows.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the SQL-level precursor to Mission 3's pandas rolling/resampling work.
portfolioEvidence.content: This rolling-average query is the direct SQL-level precursor to the resampling/rolling operations Mission 3's pandas lessons will build on top of.

labs.title: Rolling Average
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring a per-source rolling average via window function and a ROW_NUMBER()-based top-N-per-group query.

simulations.title: Sliding Window Visualizer
simulations.description: Animates a sliding window moving across an ordered row sequence, computing a rolling value at each position without collapsing rows.

flashcards.front: What's the key difference between a window function and GROUP BY?
flashcards.back: A window function computes a value across a set of related rows but still returns one row per original row. GROUP BY collapses all rows in a group into a single summary row.

flashcards.front: What does PARTITION BY source_id do inside an OVER() clause?
flashcards.back: It restarts the window's computation separately for each distinct source_id, so the rolling average (or ranking) for one source never mixes in rows from a different source.

rubric: Correctly identifies when a window function is needed instead of GROUP BY, based on whether detail rows must be preserved
PARTITION BY and ORDER BY are used correctly so the window computes per-source, time-ordered results
Rolling average / ranking results are verified against a small hand-computed example, not just assumed correct
